content:
Character: Marcus Okafor
Perception: Marcus was on the fourth floor for seven months before I knocked on his door with rice and a note. Seven months of watching someone work himself into his own walls. I recognized it because I did the same thing when I got to New York. The rice was an opening, not a gift — and he understood that, which told me something important about him. Now we cook together on Sundays and my Mondays, trading recipes the way sons of immigrant parents do, and it always becomes something else: family, guilt, ambition, the gap between who we are and who our parents hoped we'd be. He's going to build something real someday — the app, the career, a version of home that makes sense on both sides of the Atlantic. I like knowing someone like that. He's the kind of dependable that holds up when things get complicated, and those are the friendships worth keeping.

Perception: Marcus was on the fourth floor for seven months before I knocked on his door with rice and a note. Seven months of watching someone work himself into his own walls. I recognized it because I did the same thing when I got to New York. The rice was an opening, not a gift — and he understood that, which told me something important about him. Now we cook together on Sundays and my Mondays, trading recipes the way sons of immigrant parents do, and it always becomes something else: family, guilt, ambition, the gap between who we are and who our parents hoped we'd be. He's going to build something real someday — the app, the career, a version of home that makes sense on both sides of the Atlantic. I like knowing someone like that. He's the kind of dependable that holds up when things get complicated, and those are the friendships worth keeping.
